speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: A very fat man, he says, I know it was you, he says, the woman, she said there, have a spiritual meeting. And he says, you come and make a noise and it's frightened away the spirits. But then there was one, the one of the two girls, Julie and Anna, that the town's named after here. And they was down there, to home, and Julie got two big knives. And she went around the houses with these knives thrown around the area in different ways and yep, yep, yep, yep, yep, or something and had ever followed her. And she was going around and around the house out of the way. The old man says, well, shoot, you should talk in your tongues. So he sat down, she must have stayed quite a while because he sat down and got mox mox. That's where that concrete house is, Pioppi Mox Mox was the chief. He had him to come up and interpret what she said. Mox mox, he come up and looked at her. He's no Indian man, he goes to the Indians, you know. He's no Boston man, English and Indian, he couldn't understand anything like that. All the same big fools, he said, and he turned around and rode off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know Boston man?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Boston man, that's what they called a white man. All the same big fool.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: All the same big fool. All the same big fool, yeah. All the same big fool.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There was a fellow down here at Spalding telling about him being in the Army. A fellow named Powell, he's dead now, but he's telling about having to guard a negro. One of them southerners, you know, that was hostile to the white people. Well, they had reasons to be in and yet they had reasons and yet they had no more reason to be hostile to the white man than the crazy white man had towards them, but they was. A hostility between them. And this fella, he had had a guard this year, fella, out while he was policing up around the place, you know, cleaning up. And this fella, he said that that nigger made a run at him. And he said when he stopped that bender was about that fur from his neck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He says my bender was about that fur from his neck when he stopped. He said I saw him. Now see here fella, he said I haven't got a thing against you. But he says I've got this job to do and he says I'm going to do it. And he talked to him and read it out. Well from that time on this nigger was his friend. And he said they'd stand around there when there was no officers around. They'd stand and talk. And as soon as an officer would come in sight this nigger would see him first. He said oh he'd just be busy you know just to work and just for all that out policing up. Soon as the officers out of sight they'd stand and talk again. They were good friends but that nigger had to, well, I don't know what happened if there'd have been a southern man and he'd have got that close. A bunch of driggards. Well they were southern most likely would have given that gun a little boost. Because they didn't rally a negro very much back in them old times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they felt about the same way towards Negroes in the south as the whites felt about Indians out here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it similar do you think or was it different?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Very different. Well I don't think there was quite as bad against the Indians there was a nigger but then, well there was more and more whites and Indians married up. Well I don't know if any more of them mixed up than there was a nigger or not. If you had a colored television and looked at them freedom marches that there used to, oh I'm bad to see that there. There'd be every color from a cold black to a white that was nigger blood in them mixed up. Well if a white man was, if a white woman accused a nigger woman of attacking her well it would get practically too bad for him. I heard of one that they would accuse of attacking a white woman and they just took him and tied him and tied his feet up to a bumper of a car and started driving and didn't stop. The police didn't lift pretty shin bones just to worry about. So all they was, them whites were rough on black men if they come mixing up with the white women but it was all dragging together different when it went the other way. White men and black women. Well there was thousands of them white southern slave holders that had a lot of nigger women. They bred them women themselves and they raised them half -breed kids and sold them. They was a higher price than what the regular nigger was.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know, this white man is awful good but I'll tell you right now he's done, sometimes I think there wouldn't have been a hell if there hadn't been a white man. All the things that he'd done he was supposed to know better but he sure didn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In talking about religion before I was thinking was there any kind of church that the families around Arrow went to?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yeah they had a Lutheran church down there a while and the Lutherans and Methodists they used the school house for a church. They used the school house there a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that with most of the gross clauses in those folks?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Gross clauses in Wilson's and Gibs's. There was an old Gibs, the Gibs's was from Tennessee. Then there was the Barnets I forgot about. They were Virginians come from the same series that we come from there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Series?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, that's the only town that's mentioned in about half of Bland County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bland, B -L -A -N -D?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, Bland County. It's out there and there's no railroad and 20 miles of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the gross clauses, those folks were all, were they Lutherans?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, Lutherans and Methodists run heavy to Lutherans.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much revivalism in the early church?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The Lutherans, they wasn't a revival people, they taught there. They had meetings so occasionally. The Methodists were pretty much the old revivals. But back then, people had to ride horseback or walk. There were people older, like people too, that would walk four or five miles to get down to the schoolhouse to have prayer meetings. They had prayer meetings there, you know, a lot of the time.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Do you remember the old papers?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yeah, down here. That was the upper schoolhouse down here. Well, they were down there sometimes too. Yeah, they had a big revival up here. That was the cleanest revival I ever seen. They had two times a year. Everybody was converted for about two or three in the whole country. They were all made of perfection there. An awful lot of them went there back the other way though again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were those revivals like in those days?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm sure that they changed some between then and...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm sure the revivals changed some between then and now. Well, no, they... The Nazarenes, they had them just the same. They were the same as they were then. But they're saying that everybody was converted. Now that's a little unusual. Yes, I said that was the cleanest sweep that I ever seen was that place there. I always thought that they all are converted, but it seemed like there was more of respect, I think, for religion back then. Well, there's one thing, you know, they went to church a little more. They didn't have... Well, the infidels... Infidels and roads, you know. Infidels and everything else. All these other things. Everybody went to church then, infidels. Sunday afternoon. But these...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this revival at the schoolhouse?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know the schoolhouse?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, it was a wooden... This one was up here for this old concrete school out here. Now there's a wooden school out there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, they had a great revival there. They went along quite a while before they got any stirrer, and then they all went and converted. I wouldn't go up there. I told them I'd have to be late at school and go to much sleep, and I wouldn't go for a long time. And finally, finally I went up one night, and then boy, when I come back, I come back different before they went up there. After that, they kept on going, and then there was a night too cold or anything else for me to go to church after that. I was up there. Down to zero, there was a couple of... Two miles and a half, three to ride, but I went every night after that. One night up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember what happened, what it was like? What it was like, or what happened to you that changed you there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they just... They just asked the Lord to forgive your sins, and that was it. If you meant it.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, what was it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Now, Carol said he'd heard you tell about your experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it in time when some of the others went up and you thought you wasn't interested, and then all at once you wanted to be up there with them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, that was a time that I wouldn't go to church around there at that time. That one night I went up there, and I seen all them little devils that they used to be out there. All up there praying, and I had it too much for me. Yeah, I went and prayed with them. I was up on the fixed ridge. You and my brother was having a revival meeting up there, and they had the sermon, and they made their order call, and there wasn't many people from the fixed ridge there that day, and there were not many from Union. But there was a Ekman from Union and me, we was there together, and so after the preaching was over, they went to make an order call. Ekman says, let's go outside, and so I went out, and all right, we went out. I got outside, there was two fellas that walked, an old fella that was walking around out there, Jim Hutchison and Bill Patrick. We went up the toilet when they went out, and we passed by. Old Jim Hutchison, he said to Bill, you know, I said they got pretty warm in there, and he said, I got out of there. He said, you know, they might hypnotize a fella.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, he was an infidel, but he just got too warm for him, he got out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say prayer meetings, what do you mean by prayer meetings back then?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, wow. A day, and they would meet. They would meet, saying and have prayers, and a lot of them would pray, and testify, and things like that, and they had no preacher there at them times. So that would be like the Sunday service, then, more or less. It would just be, just someone would hold it without a pastor, you know, they'd just have a scripture, and they'd have someone to lead the prayer one time, maybe some other one the next, and they would have, you know, discussions, and just testimonies of the experiences that they had in their religious life. Then they didn't stand to pray like they do. You know, now they do not pray, and they were down on their knees by their seats, you know, with all the prayer meetings back then, at that time, or in prayer of any kind, people were kneeling. Now then, a lot of it, well, some kneel, but mostly, you know, they stand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the different denominations like Lutherans of Memphis, would they combine together for services like that, or would they keep it separate?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, down at Arrow, there was different, well, members of the different churches there. Now they had the Lutheran church, and the Methodist, or... Well, there was just a little community, and they all come together there. Yeah, they all kind of worshiped together, you know, as to whatever. Mostly, they were Lutheran ministers in there, but there was a Methodist minister in there for a long time, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a certain few people that led the services, usually, down there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, they had a superintendent at Sunday school, and...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He generally read that Sunday school part of it, and then the preacher come and had the other... If they had a prayer service, well, they used to have different ones, and then sometimes they'd have different ones to teach the Sunday school, a superintendent that would run the whole business, the whole church, the old school, but then they had different teachers teach different classes in the Sunday school. Well, they'd usually have a secretary and treasurer for the collection, and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find, when you were a youngster growing up, that religion was very important in your life?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That religion, was it important when you were young to you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yes. I'd look at the grandchildren and the children of today, and, you know, it's kind of hard to just see the difference and the ideas and things, you know. And it was really quite a part of my life, because, you know, I was always used to church and been a state of my grandfathers a lot, and he always had prayer morning and night, you know, family worship. And so it was quite an important part, and now, you know, there's some beliefs or some religions that don't believe in saluting the flag and things like that, and that always kind of puzzled me, because in school the flag was very important to me, but never once did it ever enter that it was before God, you know, that God was first, and that was the symbol of our country, and, you know, your freedom of the country, and love the country and all, but it always puzzled me, because never to me did I ever in any way consider, I don't know, yeah, that that flag detracts anything from God, because... Well, we claim we put on our money that in God we trust, so our country here is supposed to be a God -fearing nation, but they've done some awful dirty tricks, like the way they come here and took the Indian's land away from them, and for a little nothing, and just drove them around like there's a bunch of cattle if they want a piece of land an Indian had, and I think I got most of that figured out, it's not very good writing either, but this other's been, if it's for fun you'd like to read it or have it sometime. I really would. And if I find those other things around here sometime, I'll try to, should come across them and put them where we know where they are. You talk, you just mentioned family worship, I'm wondering what that was like in your family. When I was a child, well, they would get up on the morning and grandpa and his family, you know, my uncles and all, and there was several of them lived there that time together, and they would go on out, feed their stock, and do the necessary chores while women got breakfast, and the women would have the breakfast already, and then when the men got the chores all done, they'd come back, and we all had together in the living room, and they'd take the fireplace, and grandpa would always read from the Bible, and then meal by the chairs, you know, to have prayer, and then they'd go and eat, they did this before they ate, and at night, evening, well, it was the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They got their chores did, and evening meal was all ready, but before they ate, why?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He had a chapter, so I was a Bible, and had prayer, and there was one, my memories of my granddad, you know, he was very religious, and there's one old fellow that was a neighbor, and he said they used to go by and riding by the road, and my granddad would be out working in the field, and said he'd be plowing a while, and then he'd stop the plow, and he'd kneel down by the plow, and he said, you know, figured he was praying, and he said, I used to think that man says more prayers than anybody I know of, you know, this, any time anywhere, he might say a prayer, but he says when I got older and everything, he said, I realized that he never said a prayer he didn't need to say, and he figured it took a lot of prayer to kind of keep going, you know, things that bothered him, but he said he used to think that Mars ball done more praying than anybody he knew, but he said, I live to know, but to realize that I don't think he ever said a prayer he didn't need to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, is that to recognize that life was rough, and that you needed a lot of help to get there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I think that they did, you know, and more willing to go for help, or ask for it or something. I don't know, to me, as I've gone through the years, it seemed like there's much more respect for religion. Well, in that time, even those that weren't Christian, you know, why they didn't make fun, they would go to church. Maybe some of the guys that drank, or you might follow country rowdies and all like that, from Sunday come, they didn't work. They worked on Sunday. They would be... Back in that country, there was no work on Sunday, no work done. They might be outgrown alongside every night, but they'd be sobering in church on Sunday, even what you'd call her up next. There was no work on Sunday down in that area. They didn't even cut wood on Sunday. They had it cut on Saturday evening. Everything was done up. All that you did was your chores and your meals on Sunday and go to church. Maybe in the afternoons you might visit some sick person or something that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there wasn't too much just community fun on Sunday after church?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There really wasn't. There wasn't too much. Just some of the neighbors would maybe get together some places and they might play croquet or a little game of ball and the yard or something. You know, they'd put a bunch of other up at some home or something that way. One of the funniest games they had back in that day, back in the East, out of the Chester country back there, was playing hoe go.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They would all have a bunch of chesters or cheeky pins. And you would get out there and they'd generally be a girl and a boy would get together, you know, and they'd have their cheeky pins or her chesters. And they'd hide around this way and they'd put so many chesters in their hands.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then they'd put their own hoe that way, come back into hoe go, and they'd say, how many?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And they'd tell them to guess how many. Well, they'd guess how many chesters they had in their hand. If they guessed right, they got them all. They got the chesters. If they, well, if you had two in your hand and they guessed five, you had to give them three to make it five. So then the other one, they'd come back at you that way. And sometimes they'd have a whole handful and then I would guess what you'd done if you had ten and guessed six. I don't know just what it was anymore. I forgot what they'd done if you didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people would play it at once? Just two?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Just two. Well, they might get together and go around that way, four or five. And each one would come each time, you know, each one had their ones from late. You know, get their hands out of the way and they'd press on their hands to see if it's solid or how it was. And they'd have a lot of fun in the evening playing with them chesters. To me, I think that anyone that didn't have religious training when they were young, or kind of brought up, had really missed a lot. Because to me, I think one of the things that you're grateful for and thankful for, I make as many mistakes as anybody and, you know, fail to live up to all the Ten Commandments as good as I should. But it always seemed to me like the thing that meant the most to me was the fact that I was taught the religion, you know, about the Lord. Well, the old time Bible, that's the area, I don't know exactly how it said it, but it said it. You used to teach your children the laws, the commandments. You'd write it on the door sill and over the door and talk to the family about it all the time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, nowadays they've got it and then they taught it in the church, they taught it in the school, they taught it everywhere. But our country here called ourselves a religious nation. We're a religious nation. Well, there's 40 million that claim to be religious. Claim to be church people out of over 200 million. And now then they've got down till they won't allow the Bible to be read in the school. They say, teach a different religion. Well, now then you can go in the Bible and hunt out your different passages and find in there what you want. Well, now one of the Mormons, main religions, they believe in their prophets. And there's their main verse is, he set them in the church. Prophets and teachers and prophets and teachers and several other things there. Yeah, and speaking in tongues, speaking in tongues and prophets and teachers. Different things have a way. Each one of them fellows has his separate thing. Not every one of them is all them things, but he's put them in the different ones that have different things to do. But that's one of their main verses in the Bible, that they put that in there. And that's a Mormons. Well, others have different verses to hunt out. They can show you. But when you turn in there and read the whole thing through and you don't stop with that verse, you don't stop with them particular verses. You go right on, read the rest of it on through there. Why, I can't see anything in it that teaches people to be Catholics or Lutherans or Mormons or anything about if you read it. And then they kick it out of the school. I don't know, read. I read when I was a kid, I was in my fourth grade, had a story in there about Ulysses exploring a river. And he found a cave up on the hill. They went up there and there's a big one eyed giant up there with a bunch of sheep. This giant eat up a couple of his men and then he gave them a gallon or such a matter of wine that they had to store them at no ordinary man could drink it. He drunk that and got them in the cave back of him and drunk that and laid down and went to sleep. And they found a pole back there and hit it in the brush somewhere, until they got him to sleep. And then they dropped his eye out. And then, Ulysses, he tied his men underneath the sheep. So this fellow had to count them with his hand, you know, they went out and he couldn't see. And he put all his men underneath them sheep and he got under a big buck and rode it out by this giant and they got away that way. Now, that ain't a fantastic story to think that a man can ride under a sheep. A man is longer than a sheep. And a sheep is only about that far from the ground. Where is the room for a man to be under sheep's belly and then he'd walk out there. Now, that's the thing that was taught in the fourth grade. And then they come to the fifth. They taught all the Greek gods. The Greek, you might say, the Greek Bible was in the fifth grade. That would all come up. But our real Bible, no, no, no, no. If I was running the country there, I would put that Bible in the school as a fifth grade reader. I know when I was a kid I didn't care to hear the Bible. And if it was read at school, I'd be doing something else. I wouldn't listen to it. But there is things in there. There's things that are true and people need to know. And if I was running the show, I would put the Bible in the school as a fifth grade reader. I was going to ask you about how religious you think that the... Now, here the fellow, Lewis Soniker, he was an infidel. He was an infidel preacher. He talked all the time you see him. He was talking about the infidel. And he was talking to a fellow one time, another infidel. He said, I read the damned old Bible through last winter three times and some more of it, some part of it, some more. And he said, I didn't find a thing in there of what we could do very well without. The other fellow said, well, by Jesus Christ, he says, I wouldn't fool that much time away with it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Who did you say?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Always says I use that expression sometimes when I'm talking.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But even so, the infidels, when they get in pain or get in trouble while they moan and mourn and oh lord and all like that too, you know. I heard of an infidel. I respect anybody's religion. They know the story. And their idea and their way. The repentance and the baptism and, you know, trying to live your good life. And as far as you're going to be a Methodist or Catholic or something, I don't expect to be questioned as to... Maybe I'm wrong, but I don't expect to be asked, well, are you a Methodist or are you a Jehovah or are you, you know. Something else that will just be it. He used to have quite a, quite a infidel organization one time at a place. And as one of the fellas told him, he says, when I die, he says, if there's a hell, he says, I'll come back and tell you. So there was a young fella in town there. He was just an easygoing sort of a young fella. He never, never spun any big yarns or he never, never told any, you know, he was just an ordinary fella. Nothing to be said much about. And he come to the place one night and he said, I seen so and so down here. And he said, I was going to speak to him and he vanished. And he said, that caused more stirrup among them infidels than anything that ever happened. Well, this year, this fella wasn't a, he wasn't a hot air trailer. He was never, he never any man, any young fella to make up any stories or anything like that. But he just walked in there and told him that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was this supposed to happen around here? Huh? Is this supposed to happen around here? Was it so?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I was somewhere else. I don't know where it was. That had happened. That didn't happen right around here. But that sure, sure put the infidels to guessing.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, talking about the good and not so good ways of living, I got to ask you a little bit about moonshining in the, in the days when you weren't supposed to be, when it was illegal to, you know, to make the booze.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there, was there a fair bit of that around that you knew of, moonshining in the, in the bootlegging?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it, that moonshining, bootlegging was quite a, say, they put out some pretty good places. Down here. We know about, in fact, it was so long, not too far from what it was. Well, it's on the, it's on Albright's place. Now it was the Indian place, though. There was an old fellow had his rented and a bootlegger come, moonshiner, come down there and stayed with him. And they went up below a big spring and they went up under the, up the draw where it kind of washed out and there's a great big thornburst there. And they went from there and dug a tunnel up and fixed them in a, a steel underground way up the hill there. And there was another, the Indians used to come around and get drunk and then the old Jack Zoom off. He lived in that old house down there. The Indians had come around there. So he went over and told old Frank Henry, he says, if you're going to have them Indians drinking around here, he says, keep them over at your place. He says, I don't want them around mine. So it made this your bootlegger mad, his moonshiner mad. He was near the room and he come up and slapped the old Jack's face. Old Jack drank. He was a drinking man. All right. But when this year a bootlegger come out and slapped his face, he says, I'll squeal on you. So he went out of the house and he didn't, they watched him and he didn't go home. He took the railroad track and started down it. So they ditched her outfit and both of them took a trip to Spokane and the moonshiner, he never come back. But they was an Indian showed them the place. I don't think anybody would ever have found it if this year Indian Amos Williams had an old word was and went up there and showed them because they went up this draw being washed out with water and dicks there. And they went up into these great big thorn brush and then turned off in this tunnel. I don't know how far it was there. I seen the place my brother and his father -in -law had rented and I seen the place after he caved in. It was quite a, they ducked right away up there in mid a tunnel. You know I kind of had the idea that a lot of the good moonshine that was made in through this country was made by people from Kentucky and Tennessee because they had a family tradition of making moonshine for a long time. They could make it better than, quite a bit better than some of the people that didn't have it like say a family recipe or that kind of thing. That's what I've heard in other parts of this county. Yes, there were a lot of moonshine made. Well, they made it before when it was legal to make it, why they made it. Our tickling road of elk said they'd make it, make legal booms and sell it to cheap they couldn't afford the moonshine. Well, I know when we come west a neighbor come with us to Spokane and him and my dad bought a gallon and I brought it with him on the train. And I think it was a dollar and sixty cents if I remember right for that legal stuff and yet they were making moonshine. Now then, of course the moonshine I guess was as good as the other but then they didn't pay the, didn't pay, I don't know how much revenue they paid on it. But then that was a revenue but this one, Barnet, Jim Barnet come out here and he hauled booms from Virginia into Tennessee for eight years with a wagon hauled it at night. And a fellow warned him to get him, a couple of fellas warned him to get him a drink and he let, got a drink for two or three fellas and he said he got three indictments against him. Well, he found it out before they picked him up. I mean he told his dad what is up and he left. And he had two brothers in Iowa, he come out there and one of the brothers knew that he was in Iowa but the other one didn't.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But their father hung himself and then the one wanted to tell the other ask him well how would they ever let Jim know about that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he says I can have Jim here in two hours. So then he got in a fight with some Greek. And he said, club he likes to kill one. He come up there and they're doing the pen. Jim couldn't read and write but he was a good hearted fellow but he did like his boogers. But he come up and worked for my brother and I, getting pulled up in the hills one time. He was sore at all Greeks and his niece come out here from the east, he'd never seen her and he's awful tickled when she come but she got to go into the Greek section boss and married him. And boy that footner killed old Jim when he'd done that, that his niece would marry that Greek. And a brother come out to work one day and he was just about a bust and a -laughing. And he said Jim was talking in there and he said that history taught that the Greek was the lowest down class of people in the world next to the nigger. And he couldn't, he'd tell them what history taught and he couldn't read the words. Well but if it was nowadays with the radio and the television and all the listening, why you'd get a lot of that. Well but there wasn't any television, there wasn't a radio then, nothing like that. He didn't really know what history taught, in other words, he didn't know. Well he used to run with a fella by the name of Steffas, Bill Steffas. And I had an idea maybe Bill, Bill come west with the Barnards. I don't know what maybe he was in on the deal anyway. He must have been the one that gave Jim the information about the Greek. Well anyway that was his history taught then. No, one thing I was going to ask you about was, you know, talking about how important church was to people or community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering, would you say like besides that, was there anything else that really held the community together? You mean the signs of church? Yeah, was the church really in or was it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, yeah. Well they used to have literaries up till all that flu came in in 1918. They never had a literary organization in the country since, but up to that time they had them everywhere. And they had an awful lot of fun in there debating on some, sometimes they had pretty good things to debate on. And sometimes, well one thing they'd debate on was the horse of the cow. And then the broom, which was the most useful to man, the horse of the cow. And the broom and the dishrag, for them it was just funny. You get kids you know to debate on that there. But don't you think Ed, like when we were younger and back in Virginia, for instance, like a community, it seemed like there wouldn't be very many, apparently what you'd say, wealthy people. And there was the church, but there was a neighbor in us, and I don't know, and there was kind of a trade if you didn't have money, you could maybe trade cabbage for corn or wheat, you know, and you could trade back and forth. And I don't know whether it was because they were kind of poor, but it did seem like that for miles, that people, you know, would just come if you're sick, anybody got sick. And of course they do that a lot now, and some places do, and you do hear of it or read of it, and they come in and fix a man's field, you know, their machinery. But then, you know, they had to do it the hard way, but if he was putting up a barn or his roof needs fixing or he didn't have his winter's wood, I need to have an accident or get sick. Well for miles around, people would just come in and they'd do the work, you know, and help out. Or they'd have what you call corn husking bees or bog rolling bees, or they'd just put out the day and then women would cook the meals, everybody would gather, you know, and they'd get flying and they'd get things fixed up for the... Yeah, corn husking was a great thing. Divide a...put a corn all in a big pile and then get everybody there to chuck it, to divide it in the middle and see which ones could get to the center first. And then they found a near...anybody that found a red ear corn got to kiss a pretty girl in the house. Oh, they had a lot of fun, anyway. And lots of times they'd have, after that, they'd have a barn dance in some places. If they'd dance in people, if they wouldn't, why they didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it different?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Do you think it was much different from between there... I'd be saying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it much different between there and out here that way?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Or was it... Well, it wasn't so...I don't know whether it was so much, you know, several years back. When we first come out, I don't know that...of course we got out here among quite a few Indians, but then we know the people that knew us. But it didn't seem to me like, well, I'll take it back there when I was a kid. It was very disrespectful if anyone died. If you ever knew their friends or any of their relatives, for you not to attend the service. And the children, they took you right along, I can remember, going to many funerals. And we had to go with a team and everything, with maybe just a hack or a wagon. It might be 15 miles to where a funeral was, you know. And I used to kind of wonder, because it wouldn't be anyone only just something, one that we'd heard of. The folks didn't know, but they happened to know some of their relatives or some of their friends. So it was very disrespectful if you didn't...everybody go. And now they don't take children. In fact, if children don't go to church, or if they do, they have a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess what I'm thinking is, was there more neighborliness, do you think, back there in Virginia, than there was out here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was more neighborless, farther out, you know, branched out, rather than just your own little circle. And I don't know whether he knows this, or...but when I think about it, and maybe it's just notions of mine, it seemed to me like there was more of a closeness, you know, more like it was a big family. There wasn't as many different churches. Now, even back there, where we come from, they got four or five churches in this little community, where one church practically, you know, held all other people. They would associate and go together, you know. So, what kind of sounds like, maybe what you're thinking is that out here, there was neighborliness too, but it was a much smaller circle than back there. Back there, it went further. It included more different people. I don't know, maybe in a way, but I've got the idea that it was done more without expecting anything in return, or something, so to speak, you know, than all the neighborliness. But then, it's human nature to feel that way, if I've done something for them, and, gee, I did that years ago, and here I am, and they can't look at me now, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Out here, did you feel that the Indians were quite separate from your community?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, right at first, then, of course, I went to school with some Indian children, and, of course, I had disturbed the Indian stories coming out here when I was seven years old. I heard all these stories, so to me, it was just, the country was so different when we landed here, you know. It was a big settlement, and then, of course, I was used to going to church every Sunday with about three or four dozen kids in the class, and knowing so many people, and then I just had to get acquainted with new kids, you know, when we got out here. And then, there was enough Indians lived around, and one fact lived by a mile of us, and the old fella, and they said that he had scalps hanging in his cabin, which I guess was true. Some people said they'd seen it. Well, I was just petrified, you know, to think about around, and was so scared that I was afraid to practically step out of a house, but it didn't even matter. All them dirty stories they told about the Indians, you know how mean they was, and got out here among them, and people that heard them stories, they scared to death of them. Well, they never were among a better bunch of people in their lives than a bunch of Indians. On the train coming out. Now, if they got, some of them would get drunk, they'd get dirty sometimes. Coming out here, why, oh, I don't remember where it was, but I guess it was about halfway out. It took about four or five days on the train, and then to make the trip. And somewhere along the line, an Indian got on the train, and when I seen him, I crouched down in the seat, and I kind of covered up, and I bet you there was never a better kid on the train, traveling, or staying any quieter than I was. I was scared to death all the time that Indian was on there. I didn't hardly move, or say I'm tired, or I want a book to read, or mom do this, or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I guess one difference when I think about it, from what I know between back there and in the south and out here, you didn't have corn huskings out here, and you didn't have log rollings either, did you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They didn't have log rollings, and they didn't die in this country here in the woods, because they had no log rollings. Well, they built log houses and log barns, and the log rolling was just simply when they was, you know, getting out wood or putting up buildings, you know, helping each other out. And there was so much trade, and if you could do so much without money, it would mean, you know, maybe one person would have been fortunate enough to have a bunch of hogs, and maybe some neighbor, you know, two or three miles away hadn't had any for meat, but he might have had a lot of potatoes or corn, it's something that this other person needed. Well, then they would trade, you know, they'd find out, and they'd trade, and if they found out anybody was in need by... And like fruit, they'd give. Everybody would bring you some of what they had if they didn't have enough left for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of that out here? Did you trade much here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I think they did swap work to some extent. They had their railroads here, and they had sales for stuff here, you know. Now, they put out big orchards here, and they used to make lots of money selling fruits from a prune... Big prune orchard and prune dryers, and they used to make lots of money off of that stuff around here, you know. But they... Then it was just me and the difference... Back east, they had no sales, and very much sales of that stuff too far up in the railroad. It's funny because it sounds like what you're saying is in a way that it was more pioneering back there than out here, even though this was newer country. Yeah, it just doesn't want anybody out. They were sure willing to share, you know. One time back there, most of the people didn't have a good cup of cherries, but there's one fella that did have, and he sent out word for all of his friends to come and help to get cherries to his place. He said, I never knew I had so many friends until I had the cherries to get one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did everybody always have enough out here, as far as having, you know, different kinds of things to eat, or was there some of this trading off going on here too?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they had... After we come here, most all of us... The old fellas that settled around in here, they all had orchards and stuff, and we come here. Of course, the first one to come, I had to wait until their stuffs grew up, but they all had their old... And had cows, and most of them, you might say, was able to eat, you know, and they didn't have a lot of times much to... They liked to buy extra clothing and things like that. And they didn't have the rummage sales, or you didn't get clothing sometimes, you know, somebody would give you some that they'd outgrown, or that was good. But it sure wasn't the time of going out and living off the good will, or your yard sales, or like we do, or somebody giving us all our clothes. We get along now, I'm what we make, because... I don't know what our pension is, because we have so much gift, we sell them buy anything, maybe a pair of shoes once in a while, and... And a pair of shoes eat on, they're now out for a quarter. People pass on, you know, and they know of us, and he's a big man, and he's a big fit. We went to a yard sale yesterday, and those shirts there, we got him for 15 cents a piece, and it had to be sizes that he could wear. And mostly they're kind of on the Sunday order, but then if he needs to he can wear them for every day, because there's a lot of the time that he... I don't get much for Sunday anymore, anyway. One thing I was thinking of was butchering bees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have those here? Butchering bees?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I don't know. Well, we used to get people to come and help butcher, you know. The neighbors come and help one another, but not so big like they had back east there. Back east they let their hogs run in the hills, and they ate acorns and chestnuts. They made it to grow most of their cells on acorns and chestnuts back in the hills there, and then they'd get them in the fall and feed them up on corn a while, finish them out on corn. One year my dad had quite a bunch of pigs he turned out in the hills, and they would frost and kill the mass, as they called it, the chestnuts and acorns. There was very little of it in the hills, and them pigs didn't grow very good. And he had a short corn crop. He brought them home, and he had some corn to feed them, and they didn't know what to do with butcher what he had. So there's a poor fella lived over in the hills, and he'd come over there. My dad gave him six of the Midlands off of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Six of what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Six of the... Sides. Sides meet them. They call them Midlands, they come between the hammer and the shoulders. He gave them six of them, and he had them on his back going along over the hill. He passed an old lady's place out pretty close there, and she called him, the name was Lavender, but she called him Maladimer. She called Al and the Hall of them, hey, Maladimer, Maladimer.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She said, where'd you get them window lights? Where'd you get them window?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Window lights, yeah. Because the sides were some kind of thin. Oh, I don't suppose there's been one that thick, you know, just yearling pigs.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know, people had a... There were also many other things back them days, kind of, you know, like they made their maple sugar and then their cane molasses, and my folks would have started the winter while they'd have barrels, they used barrels for about everything, wooden barrels. No, the people should. The big barrels of cabbage. The people should now. The way things are going now, they should when they're planting a shade tree. Now, these are maples here, but they're not sugar maple. A fella should get him some sugar maple trees and put them out. He could cut down on this sugar business quite a bit by having a maple tree, sugar maples because, well, I've made a little bit of syrup out of these here, but I never had more than a gallon of water, and it takes... Oh, something like a hundred to one. You get that boiled down, boil out a hundred gallon of water and get one gallon of syrup or sugar. You know, I've heard that someone said to me that the gross clothes have brought out the original blackberries that grow in this valley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No. Well, I'm Marion. I... Marion had some regular blackberries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he bring them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Old man Kimberly. Ronald from... Old man Kimberly got that blackberry that's one... ... that grows about that high, the one kind. But these here... Well, that was wild blackberries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These, what are they called, the wild?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: These Himalayas. That's what I mean. Himalaya blackberries... My institution was supposed to have got ahold of one that started it from somebody, but she didn't bring it from the east. No, I don't know who was... No, she ordered... She ordered some thornless. Well, a thornless berry is all right. You plant them, get them and plant them. I don't even know how that is. But you plant that thornless berry and it'll be a thornless berry as long as it keeps growing from the top. But if you get that top killed back to the ground and it comes up again, it'll be a thorn. So that's what... That's what these down here by George Johnson's, them and my aunt started them there. But she got a thornless and put it in there and it died and the thorn started there. But these others, a lot of them, I don't know how they got started from one place to the other. And my brother, Will, got... had about six plants of them down in the Catholic Gulch. And he gave my sister some up on her place there where Albrash got. We put some up above her spring on the side hill. And then we took some and planted them down on Albrash. And I got some later and took them and planted them down with a railroad. I helped scatter them. But then a lot of the others, they scattered on their own some way that they got scattered through the country so much. Do you think there was wild blackberries in the country before... No, they wasn't here to limit... These were all that... There were a lot more wild berries back in Virginia than there are out here. Oh yes, there was wild black... wild black caps back there. We called them raspberries, black raspberries and red ones. Well, there's wild... there's wild... There's things. Wild black caps in this country here too. There's lots of wild strawberries. You know, people... they never got so very big but they were often good. People went out and picked them. There was so much of the living that they could just go, you know, and the huckleberries. The folks would take baskets. Everything was baskets instead of... Well... Go out early in the morning and come back just about dark. And they'd have three or four gallons of peas. We think here about going to the huckleberries when we went back. But then they lived right in the woods where the huckleberries were on, half a mile or so back from the house there. Just a little way back in the hills of where they was. They lived in the huckleberry country. The chestnut trees died out. But there used to be, you know, what they call it, chinkapins that we'd go and gather in. And then chestnuts and... The chinkapin was bigger than your little finger. Just small, nice little nut. And they grew it on a brush. It never got... I don't suppose it ever got ten feet high. Just a bunch of brush and them chinkapins grew on that. But a chestnut tree, I don't know how big they did get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they brought out some chestnut trees too, didn't they? From the South?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Because I noticed there's a lot of chestnuts around... There were a few people who got chestnut trees around in the country, but that disease went to the country back there. Well, the disease was just about the same thing as what got into the fur. I seen some chestnut poles that they had for rafters back there, and what got them was a little bug, a worm, that just went and cut the sap. Went under the main bark and cut the sap bark in two, and just cut it so many places it let kill the trees. But a thing in back as a kid, maybe it was because I was a kid, and maybe I missed the maple trees and the chestnuts and the black walnuts. Of course, they raised some black walnuts there. But when I think back, outside of just some clothes, why they made their maple syrup and they made their cane syrup, and knew everybody had a good patch of cabbage, and they'd have this greatly 50 -gallon barrel or so made up of kraut. And then we like kraut apples. Whenever they'd make the cabbage, there was kind of apples that we had there. They'd take some of those and put them down. And as the kraut worked, those apples would take on that taste, or kind of a sour, they'd get kind of wrinkly and kind of sour. And of course, they'd put the cabbage cores in the barrel too, and that would kind of sour. And I remember in the wintertime, some of the neighbors sometimes would come in, roast chestnuts on the hearth of the fireplace, and they'd eat roast chestnuts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And one neighbor, Ernest Kitz, he'd come over now and then, and he'd eat chestnuts a while, and then he'd say, well, is your kraut apples good? How about them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he'd say, well, we haven't tried them for a while. Well, let's see about your kraut apples. And they'd go get them, dig them down in the barrel and bring out, you know, a band full of those apples from all along the kraut and sit around and eat those kraut apples.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would they taste? How would they taste?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I've never had it. Well, they just kind of like sour. Just like the sour, just about the same as sauerkraut. I mean, you know, I could hardly explain it, but they'd have that kind of an acidy taste, you know, kind of like kraut does. And it wasn't every apple that would work, but a certain kind of... You had to try to find them, it wasn't wormy.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Popcorn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that something that they commonly did, had popcorn?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Or they'd raise a popcorn, you know, and then they'd have that thing and they had this big wire popper and then put it in the pot. And then the night thing, but sometimes for entertainment in the wintertime, and instead of, you know, just setting a time and having a regular party, like you might find a knock at your door, and there'd be four or five neighbors coming in with a cup of sugar. And they said, well, we just decided kind of ultimately decided to get together and come to Bosworth for a taffy pool. And maybe one would take their cup of sugar and go down to the next neighbor and they didn't have a lot of telephones, you know, and they'd say, well, come on, let's go over, you know, so -and -so. And spend the evening at McTaffy, and they would bring their sugar along, you know, and make a big batch of taffy and pull out. That's kind of the way they entertain themselves quite a bit, you know, in the wintertime. This checkers was a big sport, too, was a - Well, they played checkers. They just, you know, were checkers. They didn't have Chinese checkers. I don't even remember the domino, the main thing. Well, they'd have a checkerboard, or they'd make the fox and goose board. Yeah, they usually use grains of corn to play that. And there's those two games, and then sometimes they'd play ball in the afternoons, Saturdays or Sundays. And then croquet, croquet, croquet and checkers were the two main things that's outside of their little winter parties and just gathering in. And we had the criss -crinkles back there, and of course we kids were always thrilled to death over the criss -crinkles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, just about Christmas time, just before Christmas, while they'd be maybe four or five guys, together up together, and they'd put masks on, kind of sort of like Santa Claus, and pull along the stocking cap on. And some of them maybe could have a fiddle, you know, or banjo. And they'd come and knock on the door, and they'd come in and play a little music, you know, and say really criss -crinkles. They'd generally have a little candy in their pocket, and they'd take the candy out and kind of throw it around, and it stayed maybe half an hour or so. And they'd always try to guess who was there, or who, you know, but they'd have these masks on. And sometimes you could figure out by their voice as to who they were, and sometimes you didn't, but of course, we kids, the older ones, growing up ones, would get a kick out of it too, but we kids especially thought it was time to go in there with the criss -crinkles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you give me an idea of how much or if any of this stuff you carried over when you came out to Idaho?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know. Now I'll take it back by now. I just don't know if any of it would be that we've been back there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, no, what I mean is when you, when you, your family moved out here, did they keep doing any of the same, those things?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, none of that done after I hit Idaho. I was never in on it. You couldn't make syrup and, and... We didn't do it. Well, down the way because there was no trees, you know, and then you hear that guy. You have the apple butter stuff, but the criss -crinkles. We made apple butter and prune butter. Once in a while, we'll have taffyk pools, all right. We'll get together, you know, and I'm going to have a taffyk pool, kind of a party. But the criss -crinkles we never had, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you play party games out here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah. Party games. After party games, you grow up by leading to have squared answers and then have games where you go out and look at the stars through a colt sleeve and they pour water down the colt sleeve or, or you get to end some kind of a game. You couldn't answer the question or do it right by, you'd have to give a forefoot and then when they redeemed the forefoots, well, you didn't know what, what you might have to do. You might have to sing a song or crawl on your hands and knees or kiss all the boys in the house or something, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was, was, now, was that something that you'd done in Virginia too or was that new?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I can't remember them doing that, you know. I don't remember the kissing games. Of course, I was only seven years old, you know. I'd been kind of a little for the, kind of, can remember about the growing up, you know, but I didn't have that. Yeah, they had them back there, I remember. Well, I might have some time to find them. Before we left back there, they had a, I was just a kid, I was only nine, but then they had a party at our place and Dave Wilson, as her stepdad, he was crippled when he had a hip dislocated and one leg was going the other, so he come up to his forefoot to redeem it and they told him to kiss the prettiest girl in the house. So he run, I had to run it with my cousin, my gross clothes, and he was lame. He couldn't move, get around as fast as her and she got through the door and slammed the door and he was kind of led ahead on the count of that leg and she hit him with that door here and he had a considerable knot raised up above his eye where that door hit him when she shoved it shut. But he said, oh, Maggie says you're not that wild, I remember that well that they played. Then when they had the corn shuckens, they got the, they got a red deer corn, you had to kiss the prettiest girl in the house and, you know, they had a lot of, they had a lot of things they thought was fun. They had their quilting bees and one they'd gather together and all finish out a quilt for somebody and let a lot of the younger girls. Yeah, they'd have a quilting and when they got the quilt done, they'd take it out of the frame and then they'd throw a cat in it and they're supposed to be young people there and then they'd all shake that quilt this way in order to keep the cat in there. And then it'd be scared. Somebody just tossed a cat over on the quilt and of course the cat would be trying to get out and whoever it jumped out over. He'd get married first. He was supposed to be the first one married and they were all trying to keep the cat from jumping out over them. Oh, I don't know, they did a lot of things so it was kind of silly in the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was quilting parties, was that done out here too in Idaho when you were here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I think that they, yes, some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But not as much?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Some of the neighborhood down there, you know, they'd get together occasionally and quilt and they'd help make the apple butter. Them days, they just made up little jars and things like we do now that are jellies and butters. They have these crocs and they'd have three gallon or five gallon crocs, you know, that you put your apple butter in and you'd dish it out because you wanted it. And it was made in the big brass kittles all over the fire and people would gather and peel apples and cook them and then, you know, think about all day and when you got through, you'd have eight, ten gallon of apple butter, you know. You know, this first page here brings to mind what, well, what I didn't think about explaining about that piece of country up in there. Them first fellers come in here and they say they located the place and set out their stakes. Well, it wasn't surveyed. Well, that country up there wasn't either. But they had the state land and then they declared the reservation and they come and surveyed the reservation first. And they did out in line second. Then they went back and come with the generals of A and come up to the reservation. Well, when they come to up there where the reservation lines was, well, they was off. This news of A come and they'd be off about possibly 20 rods. The lines wouldn't be. Well, then when they come up here, they'd be, this reservation, like it started here, you see, and they'd come up here. Well, here's this strip of land in here is not, you can't make a 40. You can't make a 20. And one end will be narrower than the other. And that was what they called a disputed strip. Now, over here on the Genesee, there was 18 acres over there. It was three quarters of a mile long. That was in that disputed strip. A fellow named Davis fell a shoulder to him and he took it up and proved up on it. It was good wheat land, over about half an acre.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened with the disputed strip? Who owned it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was government land. And somebody, well, he took that up as a homestead. Then up in there, up in that country there, up a Te can country there, there was one man. Well, this French Pete, his place was so little he went off and left it. He had an orchard put in there. But I don't know just how long it was. He stayed there a long time. But the next place there, there was 80 acres in it, a mile long. It run from one to clear across a second, 80 acres. And then there was another one that joined under that. There was 80 acres, a mile long. There's two pieces there that long and there are strips. The next place then over there, he got some of that disputed strip and another piece or so, 40 or such a matter on to it, made them a place. And then there was a fellow by the name of Mapes. He got 105 acres of ground. He got some of the disputed strip and a 40 on that. I don't know if it's made him 105 acres in his place. And then there was another one, well, a ludie on beyond that. He got another piece of that disputed strip. And the lines from this side here, well, you might say your lines come here where you come down to this disputed strip. Your section line would come in here. And over here then when it went from the, over on the reservation, it would be over here. They didn't get their lines square when they come with this line from the general survey over here to this, so that's what left that narrow strip in there because it wouldn't, well, it would have been there anyway if the lines had a match, but they didn't. Now this place where Baker's got down here, that's on that disputed strip part of it there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So that strip coming in from Genesee, I don't know, clear across the country, through a particular number.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So anybody could claim it? Anybody could claim it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, they had to get a government right to it. I mean, you could just file on it. Yeah, you could file on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did that make your land run kind of funny? I mean, did you wind up having a narrow strip?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, that disputed strip that way would be narrow. On one side your corner, your section corner would be like here, and over here on the reservation side, your section corner would be here. And they didn't match across that disputed strip.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How wide would it be now, about 180 feet?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it'd run, well, this place that Davis got up there at one end of it, his place was just 50 feet wide. And the other I don't know. Well, three -quarters of a mile long and 18 acres, so you know, it was pretty narrow strip of ground through there. But these up here, Tinkin' had got wider here, so that a mile of it made 80 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, one thing I wanted to ask you about was, for your father, do you think that he trusted people that he knew from Virginia or people from the South more than he did just ordinary people, other people around here? Do you think he trusted them more?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I don't think so.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I wondered if he'd have more, you know, I just thought maybe he'd have more confidence in the people that he knew from back there. No, I don't think he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they had great old times in them there. The agent said that this fellow here says that you kept them five years, you proved up only five years. Well, you had from five to seven. If you took possession of that place and established a residence on it in six months, and then you could be off of that place, you had to stay on it two nights in six months, and you had to fence it, and you had to farm it if you could. If you couldn't farm it, of course, there's an awful lot of it you couldn't, but you had to try to farm it, and then you could change it to a stone and timber claim if you couldn't farm it at all. But you had to prove up in five years if you kept being on there a lot of time that you were supposed to be within the time. But there was two people in the county that didn't get there as much time as they were supposed to be. So they had to go seven years before they could prove up. They had from five to seven, and they got proved up all right, but they had to put in two years more time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was Eli Hickman and Bill Castle with them two, but Eli and Bill, they come down into the Catholic Gulch down there. And there was a fellow coming, took up a place down there, and he got took out for being a bootlegger. First settler in the Gulch, he went to the pan for selling booze. Here's a picture of your folks' place, and I can't find it. This is the Pix Ridge place where he's... Oh, that's nice. Selling booze to the Indians, and then Eli and Bill come and tuck it up. And this is his family, father, mother, Levi, and Will, and Albert.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one is him?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: This is Levi, Ed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He got by with it. Bill took a homestead down there where there was a big spring. Oh, it was a dandy big spring there. And Eli took his up the canyon a mile, possibly a mile further up. But they built a pretty good house on Bill's place, and Eli lived there a year or two. My brother had a place right below it. They never thought about what kind of a deal they would pull in. But then finally, Bill had forwarded his right that way. He had to get rid, so he got the old man Jamison to take... He got his place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why'd he give him for it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know. But anyway, Eli went up to his own place. That's why he'd fooled around in a couple years, why he had to hang on to his seven years.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But I think that somebody could have contested both of them and beat them if they would have known. But to prove that a man is not there tonight, so six months and so on and so on, it's pretty hard to do. But anyway, they got by with it, and they sold it. Jamison proved up on the place that Bill had homesteaded. Jamison was Eli's father -in -law.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They got that book of old Genesis. He's got quite a thing about telling about Jamison and Hickman. Eli was up there one time, and he ought to run cattle. And there was a fella bought him some winter underwear. So Eli seen it, they took it down to the livery bar and showed it to Eli. He says, that's just what I want. And up to the store he went to get him some underwear. So somebody got run ahead, and Eli went up there to get his new underwear. And when they come back down, they had to look at Eli's underwear. And the clerk had slipped around and wrapped up some wear. Some ladies' underwear, Eli. And he had to show his big heavy winter underwear. He'd drop a lady's underwear and pull off on him. He had to go back and get his other underwear after that. But they had a convention up at Moscow. All the old soldiers went up there from Genesee. And the fella that wrote that said they got a bottle back in the back of the bus where all them older soldiers was. And they'd done the talking. And he said, I don't know, I don't see how Lee ever surrendered 30 ,000 soldiers and and Appomattox from all the men that them old fellas had killed. I don't see how these old men had come from. That's Virginia. And this is Ed sitting right there. Oh, that's quite a picture. Quite a picture. I don't know. Can't tell much more about it. But his father and mother and some other men. That was not so long before they come to Idaho. That was the house in Virginia.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's quite a picture.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You know, I wanted to ask you, the way you remember some of these things from Virginia, even though you were so young, makes me realize that they were really important to the important part of life there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering, do you think that your family missed Virginia very much after they came out here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know. Of course, later in later years, my mother had two brothers that came out. And she made one trip back before my grandpa passed away to see him when he was sick. She only got to go back once, but she was pretty lonely, you know, even if there were a few Virginia families and she had no one, you know. And I think she missed it, you know, pretty much at first. And of course, I especially missed it. It was quite an adjustment for me. But there was one thing. I had asthma awfully bad back there. And the mid -summer didn't make any difference. Sometimes the doctor was at our house three times a day. And just, or asthma. So, after it comes to Idaho, I never did have an asthma attack. I've had kind of bad health, a lot of things, since the years have gone by, but never asthma.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when we went back on the visit in 50 – 53, we went back, or 52? 52, we went back, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You went back in 51, and I went in 52.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right, Ed?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: We went to Virginia in 52. And I was there about six weeks. And there was one time, one day, I don't know whether it was because I was just extra tired or what, that I kind of imagined that it might have bothered me a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I just don't know that – When you say that you missed Virginia a lot, how come?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You were just a kid at the time. Well, I think that all my – the impression was that I had so many relatives, had so many kids that I played with and everything. And then when they come, it looked to me like the end of the world when I got here. Because we landed here around the time in June, and it was real hot. And the hills were dry, you know, and I learned to screen and say, all right, now. And just had the – stepdad just had one large room cabin, you know, on his ranch that time. He built on more later. But when I come, you know, to a strange place, everything was so different. Of course, the country was different. The hills was so big. And as I said, all the stories about the Indians and – while these other Virginians were here, YSEI hadn't really known them. And their children, a lot of them were smaller than I was. So until I got started school in the fall, after we come in the summer, I didn't have much chance, didn't get acquainted with anyone. And to me, it all seemed pretty terrible, you know. I wouldn't go back to stay, you know, after I was here for a few years. But the first few years, and then I still, as I grow older, I think as you grow older, you go living in the past. And my childhood in Virginia was very important to me and my memories. I read here where this fellow built his house, started to build his house, and he's about – oh, quite a little ways off the place. Off to the place, off to his place on the neighbors when they got surveyed, and he had to move with that. Then you may think of old man Kimberly. He was the first settler in the country, and he just got in and fenced his place up there, and he – oh, he went out 20, 30 rods, or maybe more. Just put his fence way out. And when my brother Albert homesteaded his place, he went to Kimberly's line and measured a quarter out, you know, and built his house, built his cabin. I think it was about 12, maybe 14 by 16. It wasn't that big. It wasn't 14 wide, 12 or 14. Not a very big cabin, he built. But he got his land of aid, and he was down there 20 rods on the Indian land. So he had to move his house up there before that big walnut tree is there. That walnut tree was planted there, and that was planted there in 1903.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he built his house, and well, the four of them must have been built there in one. He was down on the Indian place better than a year before he got his place laid out. And then my other brother Marion, Kimberly had his fence way down on government land, and he took up his place, and he was going to investigate it there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So Kimberly got out there, and he moved the fence way back. Oh, 20 rods are better, back up the hill. But he left it about two rods, blew his house. Well, we got the surveyor in there, old Briggs, he'd surveyed it. He'd done the original surveying and all, but he surveyed out from my sister's place and set the hairline, and my brothers were on the same. And they had the compass set on this line, and Kimberly would come up and look through, looked over the compass, told Briggs, my God, he said, don't survey that line till I get out of here. You'll run me. So Briggs had business somewhere else, and my brother called him. He'd come and survey that line five or six times. I know one time I went to Arrow in the hack to meet Briggs. He was coming to survey that line. When I got down there, he said, I'm sorry, but I've got to be somewhere else today, and I can't go. And he never did survey that line till Kimberly sold out and left. And when they got the surveying, I got the place of aid, while my brother got Kimberly's house, his barn, and his spring. He got the whole business. So Kimberly proved up and never did have his house on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your brother got the house and everything? What about the guy who bought the place? What about the guy who bought the place?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He must have been pretty unhappy about that. Yes, it was. They divorced, and the woman then that they divided, Kimberly had bought out a hole, and that made him a half a section. So they divided the place, and this woman, she took her third of the place down on that side, and she remarried. And she married a girl named Thompson. My brother met him in the road, but her and her little boy covered their faces, so Mary didn't see him, and he didn't know Thompson. So he was going up to my uncle's up the road there, and I was going, and when he got back, what his family was, the family was moved into the old Kimberly house there. Thompson had moved in and took possession. So he had a heck of a time getting them out of there. He finally gave them the house, and they cleared up a place just above the line and moved it up there. Then they tried to steal the spring. The spring was only about that far from the line, just a little bit. So Thompson tried to steal it by digging under the... kind of digging right against the fence and under it. Well, I think he got more water than what my brother had, but he didn't bother his spring. There was lots of water there at that place in the hill. He got lots of water, but he didn't bother to get the spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they cover up their faces?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, because my brother wouldn't know them, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't he want your brother to know them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they were coming to move in this house that was on his place, and he didn't want them in there on that, so that was why they... It's too bad that you're... It's too bad that you couldn't have gotten it surveyed before Kimberling sold out. Oh, yeah, but I don't know... Briggs never said that Kimberling had his leg, but I think he did. I think old Ken Kimberling went to slip the 20 down in his pocket when he told him not to survey that, because I don't know why... I don't know why Briggs would be better to Kimberling than he was to my brother, but he never surveyed that line until... Back to the families just for a second here. Yes, sure. Ed is the only one left out of all of his family, and I had four uncles, six aunts, and quite a few cousins, you know, right around where we lived there, and I have one aunt left, and I don't know... And no cousins. I don't think that was there at the time that I was. I have some cousins that was born later that lived around in there, you know. To us, and, you know, when we think of all of our lives, with the aunts and the uncles, and with his family, and all, with the sister -in -laws and the brother -in -laws, and all. And just down to the two of us, and I was left out of so many memories, you know, that just he and I, the last days on the tree, you know, you might say, only I do have one aunt that's living, and she's about 80 years old. You know, I'm surprised that your memories of Virginia are as sharp as they are, because you were only seven when you came out. Well, I've often wondered how, you know, just how far back you couldn't remember things. And I know some of our children don't remember too good, even back at four years, and there's things that I do remember, but there's a lot that I don't remember. My mother got awful provoked at me a lot of times when we'd be talking about Virginia, you know, as I grew up. And she talked to me about her mother, you know, my grandmother, and I'd ask her about her, and I'd look at her picture, and she'd say, well, you ought to remember her.
