And I said, no, I don't know if there was ever such a person. And she said, well, she was so good to you, and she says, I get so mad at you because you can't remember her, because she says, you asked me questions about things that happened while she was still living. And she said, if you could remember that, you should remember your grandmother. But I think that the reason that I probably didn't, because I was around her so much, with her so much, it just wasn't anything special that impressed on my mind, you know. And I can't get to this day ever surmised that there was such a person as Mama's mother. But the things that I asked her about, when I would be three and four years old, why, she said, when Grandma's still alive. And one of them, I can remember just as well. And I think the reason was, I always was impressed by stories and pictures of trains. And, you know, I always liked the railroad, that's what we're doing off of today.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But anyway, there was a man named A. McNutt, and he had been to our house. Not many times, I haven't seen him many times, but he went somewhere, and I can't remember just how the accident happened, but he was killed by a train. And of course, a kid here in the family discussed how terrible it was. They picked up parts of the body. Part of his body was just made out of cotton. But they did have the head and the arms. And so, of course, you know, kid -like, and the train did this. And I think that that was why. And of course, they took kids to the funeral, and we went to his funeral. And I can just describe how that guy looked, you know, to his face. Because it took a special look at him, and I can remember all that, you know. And I think the idea was the train part of it. And I had never seen a train then, just hearing them talk. But his face was pretty good only. It just looked like, you know, it had three or four, maybe just like, briar scratches. They didn't get covered up. It was kind of on—I think he had two or three on one side, and then one kind of down here. It was a very nice -looking man, kind of dark hair. And another thing that impressed you about funerals, those days, I like it much better the way they do now. They bury you in the clothes you're used to wearing and put your glasses on you. And you might have some color casket beside black. But if you were a grown -up person, you had a black casket those days. And the mourners wore the veils, black veils. And men had black hat bands or black arm bands. Black, black, you know, made an awful impression on me. And I said, I never wanted to have any black about me, you know. Oh, my mother was old -time, and she talked about a black dress. And us kids would tell her, don't need to get it, because if we don't leave you, you'll never have it on, you know. So all of them thought they had to have their burying clothes. If you were young, it was white. Yes, I've seen that. But everything else was black, black, veils, black, everything. I remember going with my mother, just a little boy over there, visit old -neighbor women, you know. And while they're there, they'd always bring out their burying clothes and show them what they have. Oh, they'd always have. My mother always thought she ought to have. And we'd get her vests. She was sick quite a bit. And if we'd give her a dress or something, us kids, she'd say, well, I'll save that, you know. That's pretty nice. I'd better save that. I can't get around very good to wear it. And we'd say, you put it right on right here, because it won't be on you, you know. And when she did pass by the clothes that she had on, she had some nice stocking some of the grandchildren would give her. And she had a very beautiful slip that some of the grandchildren would give her. And I'd saved. And those things went on her. But the dress was absolutely new, kind of a gray, and so on. But I got an awful hatred of black, you know. And I just never wanted anything black about it. Tell them about the fellow killed on the railroad and cut all the pieces. I worked on the railroad, and they got to having shows. And we had a foreman was supposed to read every week something about safety. There was something to do with safety plans on the railroad. And we went to a show. John Lewis and I had all of us go down there. And they had one man in there. He was the most safety -minded man that they had on all the railroad, of all the men. He was the most careful man that they had. And he was standing, they was double -tracking. He was standing behind the caboose, talking to a bunch of men. And they told something funny. And he just whirled around like that, and another train passed, and they had a closed funeral for him. Tore him all to pieces. He just turned around to close the tracks. You know, our train had quite a suction to it. And what he laughed about was the funny he whirled around and they shook him to the train. Just took him over there and took him off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Took him where? How did he kill him?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Tucked him right under the train, you know, and just tore him all to pieces. They had a closed funeral for him. And he was the most safety -minded man that they had. Well, that there is... You know, it ain't how much you know about protecting yourself and being safe. It's what you do at a certain second that something dangerous is going to happen. That's what gets you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said a closed funeral?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, a closed funeral. They used to always open the casket while people had to go. Didn't know, but he was just tore all the pieces. They didn't, but there wasn't allowed to open the casket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you there when this happened?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'd just seen the picture. I wasn't there. He was with the railroad bunch... Showing us how to be careful. Be careful of what we'd done and showed how that man was always so particular about what he'd done. And then at that one particular time, he just whirled around and it would win that train code.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this happen to him in Lewiston or was it something else?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, it was just a picture that showed that it had happened on the line somewhere. It was showing these guys... Showing us to not get to life and then fall on their train. It was showing to them at Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were trying to say how it was really different, most different for you, your life, back there and out here, what do you think would be the main differences between there and here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it different? Was it the same?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm just curious. Well, I don't know. I first come out here at 7 and I grew up out here. After two or three years, I began to get adjusted and that thing. But still, there's something in me that while I wouldn't want to just go back there to live now, of course, naturally because we have our children and grandchildren and everything, and our families have been here and most of his folks are mine, very dear and everything. So while I wouldn't want to go back and live, that's still my memories. As I grow older, it seemed like that Virginia was home. I don't know why. It's just really hard to tell you. Then when we went back, see, we went back in 51 and I was 7 when I left there. So 7 from 11 was 44 years. It had been 44 years since, or 54, 45 years.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So when one of my aunts had built a house on Grandpa's Place and part of the old, long house, I was born in Grandpa's Place in the long house. And part of the long house was still standing on this place. The way that I was used to going in from the little town of Seawee's was a different direction than what we came in from land and stopped at Cousins and my aunt and her son came and got us to take us down to her place. Then when there was a go and I said, well, I don't remember going in this way, you know. It was always kind of from the other end.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So they were a felony who lived here, who lived there. And I said, now we're not going to say anything. And we're going to see if you recognize when we're coming to the place. So of course I was real busy looking along and interested and finally I went down the road, you know. I said, oh, there it is. And I know that route said, oh, you're right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, you sure?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You know. But of course I hadn't seen it for 44 years, but there was still enough of it there, you know, the little branch we called up, kind of a little creek that went down through the place or used to play by the creek and pick flowers along the edge of the... I didn't get to go to school but about a month back there because you had to be a certain age at school time. And the mom started me, but I kind of kicked, you know, about it because I wasn't six when I started. So all my schooling was right down here at Arrow. I just went through the eighth grade. I was almost eight years old when I started the school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember why they decided to come out when they did, to come out here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, the stepdad, he came out here and took up a homestead. And then he had known my mother, you know, and been quite interested in her. And so during the years he was out here, why then when she was free, again, why he started writing, you know, just as friends. They had been. So they took up together and decided to get married. And so he came back, you know, to marry her and come West. And so that's the way we got out here, you know, because she had known him and his family and all, and a few years before. And so then they came. And I thought that was pretty rough, too. I had to get used to a new dad and a new country. And all of them teased me about him because he was a crippled guy. He wasn't a bad guy, you know, anything like that. But when he came back, he had a beard. All of the little friends around that had here and their parents say that was to be my new dad and so forth, and they teased me about him, you know. Well, when he was crippled and had that beard, I didn't believe, you know, that he was coming until he got there and everything. And of course he shaved soon after he got there, you know, trying to be Wild West, I suppose. But anyhow, I thought my mother belonged pretty much to me. So I have sympathy for step -children or step -parents, you know, I have step -parents. A lot of them are good and everything, but if you're old enough to, I think it's better if you get them when you're just real tiny. And it's always kind of hard to, of course, I just have the half -brothers and sisters. I have a one sister, several half -brothers, and I'm eight years older than this second family. Of course, they don't know the difference, and I really don't realize it, but I was big enough to be a lot of help because there was only a year or two years or the most, a year and a half, and all between them. And there was seven, six boys and a girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a big hand in raising them? Have what? Did you have a big hand in raising them? Me?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I have a big hand. Well, I don't know. I came in a very good babysitter, I think, being eight years old, you know, when I started coming because I could do a lot of things, you know, that could be helpful. And as each one of us older, I would stay out of school with my mother. She was sick quite a bit, and I'd have to spend a lot of time out of school kind of helping at home. I always wanted to milk cow, and I just loved to milk. I never got to milk very much before I kind of died at home because, you know, mom would say, oh, well, you'll kind of spoil the cows, you know, about giving them down the milk. So she didn't want me fooling with her cow or anybody else as much. And when I got to wanting to milk out here, while my stepdad let me start, she said, well, now, she might cause the cow to dry up well, well, so she wants to learn. She has to learn sometimes. Well, I thought it was wonderful, you know. I always did like to milk, though. But after I learned to milk, that became my job, bringing in the cows and milking along with my baby sitting and all that. Some work. Work for a young girl, that's quite a good work. We had to carry water sometimes from the river or carry it from a spring. And I carried water for a mile just to drink because the spring was up in some thorn bushes and in the hot weather, it seemed like it would get so kind of taste like the leaves or something, you know, from the thorn bushes. And a mile away, they had a dandy well, good cold water. And we'd go late in the evening with my big pail and I'd carry a pail of water and then we'd set that in the cellar and go in there, you know, for our drinking water. But then the other water line for other usage, we carried from the river, carried from the spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you do that regularly in the evenings?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Quite regularly, you know, during the hot weather, you know, we'd go, oh, maybe, maybe I wouldn't have to go, but every other evening or something like that, you know, just whenever the water would begin to get low. And there was other neighbors that went with me, you know, sometimes too. And I started washing baby pants when the first half brother was born. I was only eight. I wasn't nine years old when I started that. I was eight in February and he was born in June.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And stepdad, he'd kind of do the washing, but he didn't like to do the first, through the first waters. And so I wanted an umbrella. Of course, they didn't have much money to afford anything. And going to school, most of the kids, you know, had an umbrella like rain or even sun and weather. And I thought I was clear out of the picture because that's about the only one that didn't have an umbrella. So I began to beg for an umbrella. And he told me that if I'd washed the pants through the first water, he'd get me an umbrella. I often wish I could remember what they looked like. I guess I must have got a lot out because he took it from there, but then he was very willing for me to. But he got my umbrella. I was so proud. I wouldn't have lasted for years and it cost seventy five cents. And now today you think, oh my goodness, you'd have to do all that and wait. They couldn't even dig up seventy five cents. But I'm telling you, there were just lots of times then that they couldn't dig up seventy five cents and have any bread on the table. You know, the bad way. So I've often thought of that. That's how important. I think it's the things that you get on your mind that really make an impression. And I like my remembering. Something that was unusual that happened or something that made me real happy or impressed me. And because there's lots of things that I don't remember that did happen in among the things that I do remember. I think it's true. You tend to remember something that's important one way or another. Something that just really makes an impression on you for some reason.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I lived around with my grandparents and all because my mother worked out. And so she worked and paid, you know, on groceries and things. And they took care of me a lot while she went out and worked. And that's why I felt pretty possessive of her. I was always happy when she came home. And we were always real close. I come pretty much first in her life too, you know, really. So I came in pretty handy for the second. Between you and your mother, did she demand obedience from you a lot or was it more of a loving kind of a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she very strict with you, I guess, is what I'm thinking, or was it more just loving?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, not... No, not... I wouldn't say strict. And yet she made me mine too. Now, like, you know, kids had the mind them days, you know, more. And it didn't seem like it hurt you like it does kids now if they're made to do something they don't want to. But if mama told me not to do something, and I did it while she'd spanked me, but there never was anything very out of the way. And she'd get pretty aggravated at me if she, you know, she didn't always just baby me or him or me either. I know that I had awful thin hair. And a lot of the people said, well, if you cut her hair, you know, maybe it'd cause it to be thick. Well, I'd heard people discuss me and my little short pigtails and how maybe if they just cut my hair, it would thicken up. And I never have had very much hair. So I decided that I guess I better have my hair cut myself, you know. Mama says, well, I don't think they're anxious to do it. She says, you look pretty funny because kids didn't go around with their hair cut, you know, girls them days. They said, you know, they wore the braid. And I, they did cut it. So finally she did. She set me up on a stool and she cut my braids off. And then she trimmed it. She cut it fairly short. I thought I was pretty important until I took a look in the glass. And when I looked in the looking glass, I ran upstairs and crawled in bed and pulled the cover up over my head. I wasn't too awful long until supper time. And mom didn't pay attention to me. I come supper time wise. She hollered at me, come on to supper. I didn't come to supper. And some of them said, well, we better go get her. And mom said, I guess if she gets hungry enough, she'll get up. And so I always thought so much of one of my uncles. And then they said, I'll meet supper. Supper was over. I was still laying up there with my head covered up, you know. And mom wasn't going to let me figure it out on my own. And my uncle come up and pull the cover back that I just let my face out, you know. He says, now listen. He said, you wanted this done. And he said, you must come on down and eat. He said, well, look at me. He said, I don't have a haircut very often. And he said, even if you grow a beard, it takes quite a while.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: And he said, can't you realize?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He says, you just can't lay here in bed until that hair grows out. He said, you'll just have to forget about it now. And he says, it'll grow faster. And I said, you just come on and eat. And he explained it all to me and I got up and went down the stairs and forgot about my bobbed hair, you know. But she wasn't too much to pimp me that way. But then she told me, she said, if you ever get into fights, she doesn't want me fighting.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: She said, if you ever fight, why?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She says, I'll spank you. Well, I have a cousin that lived there with us. She was a year older than me. She was a different turned person. She was quiet and kind and she wouldn't hold up for herself or anything else. And I was just one that didn't take only so much until I would look out for me. And so there were some neighbors that lived up on kind of a little hill from us. And there was an apple tree just not far from their yard. But the apple tree belonged to the place that the folks had rented, our life of people. And there was a few other apple trees on the place, but they were later apples. And the people that owned the place had been used to letting the kids just have all the apples they wanted from this tree up on the hill. And so did our folks let them have apples. But the kids, there were two or three kids' kids. And we all liked apple dumplings. So one evening, the folks told us, I don't know, you take your little pails and you go up and you pick up some apples off the ground from under that tree because they were earlier and they said, I think they'll be good for dumplings. And we don't have apple dumplings. So we were thrilled, the idea of apple dumplings. We took our pails and went flying up the hill, picking up apples. So pretty soon these kids came out through the fence and over to the tree. And they said, you just better get out of here and leave our apples alone. And for the either kind way, she said, well, the folks sent us after them. We're only taking a few off the ground, you know. And said, you can still have apples. And we're not stealing your apples. Oh, yes, we were stealing their apples and they was going to knock the turner pails over. I had to start trying to talk to them. And there was kind of a junk pile not very far away. Had rocks and old shoes and just, you know, knocked out their junk. And so when Ida was trying to tell them, you know, that one of them pushed the pail over and the other one grabbed up an old shoe and throwed it at Ida. Or she didn't try to fight back. So I thought it was about time I got into it that they were going to start fighting, you know. And so I told them that there wasn't their apples and that we'd been sent after them. And I got to throwing shoes and rocks, too. So when we got home, Ida was scared, you know, and just telling them, oh, well, it always told me I got a lickin', but that didn't matter. Well, I was mad by how I was going to see if they didn't get the best of her. And so when I told the whole story, I said, well, the kids just didn't understand, you know, that the apple tree really did belong to the place. And of course, they'd always let them have apples. We always get neighbors played together. But these kids, our kids are, they decided that we was taking something along to them. And so Mama said, well, after supper, she said, I'll give you a spankin', because she says, I told you never to fight. And so she gave me the spankin'. And then my uncle had talked me into gettin' up from my hair to grow up. Why, he took her aside and he said, well, it's all right for her to mind you, but nevertheless, he said, she was kind of with it or her rights, too. And he said, I don't think you should have spanked her. He said, I think you should have just talked it out. So of course, naturally, I thought that was pretty important, but I had some allies, bad moves of mine. But Mama and I were always awful close, and she was very, very loving, you know, and with me. And she would, as I grew up and began to get older, she gave me quite a lot of freedom. She talked to me, talked me a lot, too. And my stepdad would get mad if I'd go out some boyfriend, and he'd want Mama to just raise the roof. And she said, I think we'd better go just a little bit easy. I said, if we just kind of keep out of it and let her alone, I said, I think pretty soon she herself will decide that she wants the more of him. And that's usually the way it worked out. And then she'd tell me, now, if you go anywhere or go and do anything, she says, I want to know. She said, I don't want to let you go to some neighbor girl's house to spend the night and think you're there, and then you'd be out somewhere else. And she said, as long as you play ball with me, let me know where you are and what you're doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And why?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She said, we won't have troubles. And so we had very few, you know, at once. Because I would let her, would tell her. And if I went somewhere and for some reason that I did go some other place for a while with the family from there, why I'd always tell her about it. I'm speaking of going some.
